header.stance: Front-led Treasury selloff as energy pressure lifts policy risk while broader risk stays orderly
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 21 Treasury close extended the prior session's energy-led selloff, but the curve changed the message. The 10-year yield closed three basis points higher at 4.63%, its highest area in about two months, as oil's advance kept an inflation shock in play. Unlike Monday's bear steepening, the front end led Tuesday's losses and the curve flattened. The market was assigning more weight to the risk that the Federal Reserve responds to persistent energy pressure, even as long-duration investors continued to demand an unusually high real return.

The energy constraint identified at the prior close therefore persisted and intensified. Brent settled at $91.78 after a 2.87% daily rise as the US-Iran conflict entered a tenth consecutive day and Houthi threats kept the Red Sea shipping route in focus. That combination extended the possible transmission from crude into fuel, freight, inventories and inflation expectations. Interest-rate futures placed the chance of a Fed increase at the following week's meeting at roughly 25%. The probability remained well below levels seen earlier in July, yet its recent rise was enough to make the two-year sector more vulnerable than the long bond.

Inflation compensation explained only part of the move. The 10-year real yield rose to 2.37%, while the corresponding breakeven increased by less. Positive real wage growth and broadly stable state labor conditions supplied a domestic reason for the real-rate component to remain firm: household income was still advancing faster than consumer prices, and the labor market had not produced a broad easing signal. Oil was the immediate catalyst, but resilience and uncertainty over the Fed's reaction function determined how forcefully that catalyst reached nominal yields.

Risk assets resisted a generalized tightening interpretation. Semiconductor shares rebounded sharply, the Nasdaq 100 advanced and volatility fell, even as Treasuries, investment-grade total returns and the dollar moved in a restrictive direction. Beneath that calm, distressed borrowers faced acute problems with liquidity, collateral and refinancing structure. Global duration added another layer through UK fiscal uncertainty and Japan's unresolved funding plans. The close is best understood as a front-led policy repricing inside an orderly risk environment: energy raised the inflation boundary, real yields kept the duration hurdle high, and borrower-specific balance sheets absorbed the credit damage.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury yields rose across the coupon curve, with losses diminishing by maturity. The two-year closed at 4.26%, up five basis points; the five-year rose four basis points to 4.37%; the 10-year increased three to 4.63%; and the 30-year added two to 5.13%. This progression reversed Monday's bear steepening and produced a bear flattening. Oil remained the common shock, but the day-to-day ordering showed that the incremental adjustment had moved toward the expected policy path.

The 2s10s slope narrowed two basis points to 37, and 2s30s narrowed three to 87. The 2s5s10s butterfly was unchanged at minus 15, while 2s10s30s declined one basis point to minus 13. Stability in the first fly indicates that the two-, five- and 10-year points cheapened in an orderly sequence. The 30-year's relative outperformance also suggests that Tuesday did not add a fresh, discrete fiscal-term-premium shock to the US curve, despite an already demanding long-end level.

Longer horizons preserve the tension between near-term policy and accumulated duration pressure. Over one week, yields were eight, six, five and five basis points higher at two, five, 10 and 30 years. Over one month, the increases were seven, 14, 17 and 23 basis points. Tuesday's flattening put the most recent adjustment at the front end, yet the monthly pattern still steepened with maturity. The market has simultaneously rebuilt near-meeting risk and raised the structural compensation attached to real growth, fiscal borrowing and capital demand.

Inflation-linked Treasuries divided the nominal move into a dominant real component and a smaller inflation component. The 10-year real yield increased two basis points to 2.37%, while the 10-year breakeven rose one to 2.26%. Five-year real yield climbed four basis points to 2.09%, seven-year rose three to 2.22%, 20-year added one to 2.71%, and 30-year was unchanged at 2.91%. The stronger move in short and intermediate real yields aligns with the front-led nominal selloff. Oil lifted inflation compensation, but the market also increased the real discount rate attached to a potentially firmer policy path.

The energy channel had both a geopolitical trigger and a market amplification mechanism. US and Iranian strikes continued for a tenth day, while Houthi threats to Red Sea traffic pushed Brent toward $92. The 10-year yield crossed the closely watched 4.60% area and the two-year moved above 4.20%, levels that rates participants identified as adding technical momentum in thin summer conditions. SOFR futures and options activity on Monday had run at 74% and 54% of their respective 20-day averages, making a fundamentally coherent move more vulnerable to acceleration.

Fed pricing captured the shift without making an increase at the July 28-29 meeting the central outcome. Interest-rate futures indicated roughly a 25% chance of a rise at that meeting, up somewhat over recent days but below the coin-toss probability seen earlier in the month. The rally after softer inflation data had been erased. Market pricing was responding to the possibility that sustained energy costs interrupt disinflation and alter the reaction function, not to a realized inflation release showing that such pass-through had already occurred.

Domestic labor evidence limited the scope for a growth-based counter-rally. Median weekly earnings for 120.9 million full-time wage and salary workers were $1,251 in the second quarter, 4.6% above a year earlier against a 3.9% increase in consumer prices. On a seasonally adjusted basis, median earnings rose to $1,258 from $1,233 in the first quarter. Positive real wage growth supports purchasing power and gives the Fed less reason to treat an external energy shock as arriving into an already collapsing demand environment.

State data offered a similar message of stability without broad acceleration. June unemployment rates fell in eight states, rose in two and were stable in 40 states and the District of Columbia; the national rate was little changed at 4.2%. Payrolls increased significantly in three states, declined in one and were essentially unchanged elsewhere. The narrow breadth of changes is not a fresh overheating signal, but it also withholds the widespread labor easing that could have offset the oil shock at the front end.

Very short Treasury yields remained far below coupon yields and moved unevenly. The four-week coupon-equivalent yield was unchanged at 3.70%, while six- and eight-week yields closed at 3.73% and 3.72%, up three and one basis points. Thirteen-, 17-, 26- and 52-week yields ended at 3.83%, 3.89%, 3.99% and 4.07%, rising one, two, two and four basis points. The larger change at one year was directionally consistent with greater policy uncertainty; the stable four-week point kept the move distinct from immediate cash-market stress.

The rates close consequently strengthened one part of Monday's thesis and narrowed another. Energy remained the clearest catalyst, and high real yields still defined the cost of holding duration. The curve no longer pointed to a fresh daily increase in long-end fiscal compensation, however. Tuesday transferred the marginal pressure toward the Fed-sensitive front end, leaving subsequent inflation and activity data to determine whether the move represents a durable policy repricing or a thin-market overshoot around volatile oil.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Broad credit total returns were orderly and duration-sensitive. LQD fell 0.28% to $106.85 as Treasury yields rose, while HYG slipped 0.04% to $79.65 and JNK declined 0.05% to $95.90. The much smaller high-yield moves fit the concurrent equity rally and lower volatility. Tuesday did not produce an indiscriminate withdrawal from corporate risk; the pressure concentrated in borrowers whose operating models require continuous funding or whose documentation allows new capital to alter creditor priority.

Aston Martin showed how liquidity access can become a contest over collateral before a financing is completed. Holders of a majority of its $1.85 billion equivalent of 2029 bonds sent a legal letter after the automaker discussed financing backed by assets placed beyond existing creditors' reach. The group argued that moving collateral, including intellectual property, would breach New York-law documents and gave the company 48 hours to reject such a transaction. Product delays, weak Chinese demand, quality problems and US tariffs had already forced the company to rely on £50 million of shareholder debt in April. New money was available in principle, but its proposed seniority threatened the recovery position of legacy bonds.

America's Car-Mart illustrated the harsher endpoint of dependence on recurring securitization. The subprime auto dealer borrowed roughly $2.5 billion through asset-backed bonds from 2022 through 2024, then tightened underwriting as consumer losses rose. Lower originations reduced sales and trapped collections in securitization trusts, weakening the cash available to replenish inventory. The company reported a 52% inventory decline and a 27% fall in car sales, after closing 42 of 136 dealerships as a warehouse facility failed to materialize.

An expensive private loan did not repair that funding loop. A five-year, $300 million Silver Point facility priced at SOFR plus 7.50%, carried warrants for as much as 10% of the equity and imposed tight control triggers. Car-Mart defaulted on it in June and was considering asset sales or a wind-down, in or out of bankruptcy. The case connects higher policy rates to consumer credit through both borrower performance and financing velocity: once originations slowed, the ABS structure forced deleveraging and made shrinking operations weaken the platform needed to restart lending.

Other stressed credits showed the same emphasis on claim hierarchy. Victoria had support from holders of more than 75% of its 2028 notes for a restructuring that would cut debt by about £300 million, yet remained below the 90% threshold for a consensual deal. Existing holders were offered second-lien bonds due in 2031 worth 75% of face value plus about 19% of the equity. Millikin University's delayed $49 million bond sought stronger protection by adding rights to a 600-bed apartment complex, even though that asset had lost about $10 million over the seven years through June 2026. In both cases, collateral and voting thresholds carried as much information as the headline financing amount.

The broader refinancing boundary is becoming more relevant as rates remain elevated. Oaktree identified about $200 billion of debt across 250 borrowers yielding more than 15% or priced below 90 cents on the dollar. That estimate is a forward-looking distress pool, not a realized default total, but it identifies the cohort most exposed if real yields and policy rates remain high into maturity dates. Credit absorption remains available for stronger structures; weaker borrowers are paying through yield, equity dilution, collateral transfer or creditor control long before broad high-yield gauges show systemic stress.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

UK fiscal uncertainty remained the strongest developed-market source of term-premium pressure. Gilts struggled after Prime Minister Andy Burnham's call for flexibility within borrowing and spending rules, and 30-year yields reached their highest since mid-May before easing. Britain already carried the highest borrowing costs in the Group of 10 and public debt near the size of annual output. Chancellor John Healey was regarded as a stabilizing appointment, but a planned household-energy tax cut costing £850 million and prior support for higher defense spending left the autumn budget responsible for establishing credible funding.

The gilt move mattered to Treasuries through competition for global duration and sensitivity to fiscal arithmetic, although Tuesday's US flattening showed that the cross-border impulse was no longer intensifying at the long end. Sterling's 0.4% decline to below $1.34 added a currency warning: investors were not treating fiscal flexibility as unambiguously growth-enhancing. Oil simultaneously pressured bonds across Europe, linking UK-specific supply uncertainty to a broader inflation premium.

Euro-area bank lending supplied the principal counterforce. Banks tightened standards for companies, mortgages and consumer credit in the second quarter as geopolitical and energy risks reduced tolerance for weaker borrowers. Corporate loan demand rose slightly because firms needed inventories, working capital, fixed investment and refinancing, while housing-loan demand declined markedly amid weaker confidence and high rates. Ahead of the ECB's two-day meeting, the survey showed restriction already passing through bank balance sheets even as officials assessed whether higher energy costs require another increase after June's quarter-point move.

Japan combined an unresolved fiscal burden with evidence that higher yields were rebuilding a domestic buyer base. The government's 14-year growth strategy called for ¥370 trillion of investment but did not settle how to fund a likely food-tax reduction or increased defense spending. Suspending the 8% food levy for two years was estimated to cost about ¥5 trillion annually. Language protecting Bank of Japan autonomy reduced concern over direct political restraint on policy, yet removal of the plan's longstanding reference to fiscal consolidation kept the long-run issuance question open.

Demand offered a meaningful limit to that concern. Japanese insurers bought a net ¥630.5 billion of bonds with original maturities beyond 10 years in June, their largest purchase in three years. The buying followed a peak in super-long yields and proposals to bring JGBs into tax-advantaged household accounts or encourage more domestic investment. Overseas investors, by contrast, sold the most two- to five-year government notes since December 2022 after the BOJ's June increase. The split suggests that high long yields can attract liability-driven demand even as expected policy normalization weakens the front end.

The yen exposed the unresolved interaction. It fell as low as 163.24 per dollar as higher US yields, oil costs and Japan's fiscal stance overwhelmed official warnings and the ¥11.73 trillion spent on intervention between April 28 and May 27. A weak currency raises imported inflation and can strengthen the case for BOJ action, while a higher domestic yield hurdle can redirect Japanese savings from overseas bonds. Japan therefore transmitted into Treasuries through both currency pressure and the prospective allocation of its large domestic buyer base.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt held up despite a slightly firmer dollar and higher US real yields. EMB gained 0.01% to $95.38, while the Dollar Index rose 0.19% to 101.184. The small fund move masked a wide country distribution. Energy exporters with stronger domestic refining or improving external accounts gained some insulation; importers and borrowers seeking fresh foreign-currency funding faced higher subsidy, current-account and refinancing hurdles.

Argentina delivered the clearest sovereign credit improvement. Moody's raised the rating to B3 from Caa1 with a positive outlook, the country's third upgrade in less than three months. Fiscal surpluses, stronger exports, energy and mining investment, improved financing access and more than $10 billion of central-bank foreign-currency purchases in the first half supported the decision. Sovereign spreads stood near 400 basis points over Treasuries, their lowest in eight years. The upgrade broadens the potential institutional buyer pool, though the rating remains deeply speculative and policy continuity through the 2027 election still governs further progress.

Indonesia pursued diversification from a more pressured starting point. The sovereign began marketing three- and five-year panda bonds totaling as much as 7 billion yuan, with approval for as much as 30 billion yuan over two years. It had already raised around $7 billion in non-dollar currencies during 2026. Thursday's bookbuilding can establish a domestic Chinese benchmark and reduce reliance on dollar issuance, but record rupiah weakness, a wider current-account deficit, high energy costs and negative outlooks from Moody's and Fitch keep the strategy tied to policy credibility.

Danantara added a quasi-sovereign extension to that test. The Indonesian sovereign wealth fund mandated banks for possible 20- and 30-year senior dollar notes after raising $1.5 billion through five- and 10-year bonds in June, above its initial $1 billion target. Fitch rated the proposed debt BBB and Moody's Baa2. Longer maturities would deepen access, yet they also expose investors more directly to the state-centric policy agenda and persistent energy burden that have weighed on Indonesian assets. Sovereign diversification and quasi-sovereign dollar duration are therefore proceeding in parallel, with different buyer bases and risk horizons.

Nigeria's central bank demonstrated how oil exposure does not automatically permit easier policy. It held the benchmark at 26.5%, naming Middle East hostilities as the largest outlook risk. Domestic refining and net petroleum-product exports offer protection, and June inflation remained at 15.9% against expectations of an increase. The committee nevertheless chose caution to protect price and financial stability. The decision preserves currency support and delays relief for domestic borrowers until disinflation is durable enough to outweigh the external shock.

Russia showed a more acute debt-management conflict. The Finance Ministry halted OFZ auctions after canceling a July 8 sale and failing to receive acceptable bids the following week. It had sold only 10 billion rubles during July against a quarterly issuance target of 1.5 trillion rubles, while the first-half deficit reached 5.7 trillion rubles, above the 3.8 trillion budgeted for the year. Suspending fixed-rate supply supported the local bond index on Tuesday, but it defers funding toward alternative sources, floating-rate notes or later issuance as the central bank signals diminishing room to ease from 14.25%.

These cases separate dollar liquidity from sovereign solvency and local-market absorption. Argentina's external profile and buyer eligibility improved, Indonesia widened its currency and maturity options, and Nigeria preserved a high policy defense. Russia faced unacceptable domestic funding terms despite control over its issuance calendar. A stable broad EM debt gauge therefore coexisted with meaningful changes in ratings, currency composition, central-bank restraint and auction viability.

## Cross asset

US equities directly complicated the restrictive rates signal. The S&P 500 gained 0.89% to 7,509.20 and the Nasdaq 100 rose 1.93% to 29,155.18. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index jumped 5.21% after a sharp prior drawdown, while the VIX fell 8.58% to 17.05. Investors renewed exposure to beaten-down chipmakers even as oil and yields rose, showing that the Treasury loss was not driven by a broad flight from risk or forced liquidation.

The rebound did not settle the longer-running AI financing question. The semiconductor index remained 2.41% lower over one week and 13.85% lower over one month; the Nasdaq 100 was down 4.11% over the month. Current buying reflected improved demand for depressed technology shares, while coming earnings still had to justify capital spending and elevated valuations. For fixed income, stronger equities reduce immediate haven demand, but uncertainty over the payoff from AI investment keeps the real-rate and corporate-supply implications open.

Commodities and currencies aligned more clearly with the bond selloff. WTI rose 1.92% to $84.83 and Brent advanced 2.87% to $91.78. The Dollar Index gained 0.19%, reinforcing the tightening impulse for foreign-currency borrowers. Gold increased 1.87% to $4,085.20 despite higher real yields and a firmer dollar, indicating that geopolitical hedging and dip buying remained active. The simultaneous rise in bullion and equities argues against a single conventional risk regime.

Credit gauges completed the mixed picture. Investment-grade total returns fell with duration, high-yield funds were almost unchanged, and EMB edged higher. Energy was the most coherent link across markets: it lifted inflation concern, supported the dollar and pressured nominal bonds. Equities and volatility refused to validate a systemic shock. The unresolved issue is whether risk appetite can remain insulated if oil stays elevated long enough to reach realized inflation, policy decisions and borrower cash flows.

## Forward Market Significance

June new-home sales on July 24, advance durable-goods orders on July 27 and advance economic indicators on July 28 lead directly into the July 28-29 FOMC meeting. Broad resilience in housing and capital-goods demand, especially if energy remains near Tuesday's levels, would keep the front end vulnerable and support the interpretation that the oil shock is arriving into sufficient domestic strength for policy risk to rise. Weak rate-sensitive demand and softer forward trade signals would challenge the bear flattening if they pull the two-year yield and real yields lower without a renewed rise in breakevens. The policy statement then determines whether officials emphasize realized inflation or the growth restraint already delivered by high borrowing costs; two-year Treasuries, one-year bills, the 2s10s slope and the dollar are the clearest transmission points.

The ECB's imminent two-day meeting follows a quarter-point increase in June and arrives as banks report tighter corporate and household credit standards. Language centered on geopolitical inflation risk and another prospective increase would reinforce the global front-end burden, particularly if Brent remains above $90 and euro-area bank funding costs rise. Greater emphasis on falling housing-loan demand, lower risk tolerance and the restraint already visible in credit creation would provide a duration counterweight even if policy is held unchanged. Bund and gilt front ends, euro-area inflation compensation, bank credit and the Treasury long end will show whether Europe is exporting another inflation premium or a growth drag; the former confirms Tuesday's energy thesis, while the latter challenges its persistence beyond the US curve.

Indonesia's panda-bond bookbuilding on Thursday and Danantara's sounding for 20- and 30-year dollar notes provide a direct test of sovereign and quasi-sovereign absorption under high oil prices and a firm dollar. Strong yuan demand near the targeted size, followed by credible interest in Danantara's long maturities without a material pricing revision, would confirm that funding diversification is broadening the buyer base despite rupiah weakness. A reduced panda transaction, heavy concession or delayed long-dollar sale would reopen concern that negative rating outlooks, the current-account deficit and state-directed investment are constraining access. The rupiah, Indonesian sovereign yields, Danantara's existing five- and 10-year bonds and regional dollar issuance are the sensitive channels; the result will separate strategic diversification from financing that depends on unusually expensive terms.

section_summaries.market_read: The close raises near-term policy sensitivity without confirming a broad risk-off regime.
section_summaries.next_check: Whether oil pressure reaches realized inflation will determine if front-end weakness persists.
section_summaries.summary: The Treasury selloff shifted from long-end pressure to Fed-sensitive weakness as oil extended the inflation threat. Rising real yields reinforced the duration burden, yet stronger equities, lower volatility, and concentrated borrower distress kept the close consistent with orderly policy repricing rather than generalized financial stress.

section_summaries.market_read: Rates repricing is concentrated in policy sensitivity, with long-end pressure no longer intensifying.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether incoming demand data lower two-year and real yields without lifting breakevens.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Coupon yields rose in declining increments by maturity, producing a bear flattening as the front end absorbed more policy risk. Real yields supplied most of the nominal move, while stable curve flies and long-bond outperformance argued against a fresh US fiscal-premium shock despite elevated structural duration compensation.

section_summaries.market_read: Credit risk is idiosyncratic and structural, not yet an indiscriminate withdrawal of liquidity.
section_summaries.next_check: Track whether distressed issuers secure funding without weakening legacy creditor claims.
section_summaries.summary: Broad credit remained orderly, with duration explaining investment-grade weakness and risk appetite containing high-yield moves. Stress instead centered on borrowers dependent on recurring financing, where collateral transfers, creditor priority, securitization cash traps, and costly rescue capital threatened recovery values before broad gauges signaled systemic strain.

section_summaries.market_read: Global duration faces fiscal supply pressure, partly limited by tighter credit and domestic demand.
section_summaries.next_check: The next signal is whether Europe exports inflation premium or growth restraint into Treasuries.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: UK fiscal uncertainty sustained global term-premium pressure, while euro-area credit tightening offered a growth counterweight to energy inflation. Japan's unresolved funding plans and weak currency contrasted with renewed domestic demand for super-long bonds, leaving cross-border duration exposed to both fiscal supply and shifting investor allocation.

section_summaries.market_read: Stable aggregate EM performance masks widening differences in funding access and policy credibility.
section_summaries.next_check: Indonesia's upcoming books will test whether diversified funding offsets currency and oil pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: EM debt stayed broadly stable despite a firmer dollar and higher US real yields, but country outcomes diverged sharply. Improved external credibility aided Argentina, Indonesia tested funding diversification, Nigeria preserved tight policy defenses, and Russia deferred issuance after unacceptable bids, separating broad liquidity from local absorption risk.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross assets validate an inflation impulse but reject a broad liquidation or flight-to-quality signal.
section_summaries.next_check: Risk insulation depends on whether persistent oil pressure reaches policy decisions and cash flows.
section_summaries.summary: Equities rallied and volatility fell even as oil, the dollar, real yields, and nominal Treasury yields tightened financial conditions. Gold also advanced, leaving no single conventional risk regime; energy remained the coherent transmission channel, while resilient risk appetite limited haven demand and deferred evidence of systemic stress.

section_summaries.market_read: The thesis now depends on policy and funding tests converting current pressure into durable repricing.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch US front-end rates, European duration, and Indonesian books for confirmation or reversal.
section_summaries.summary: Upcoming US activity data and the FOMC will test whether energy pressure meets enough domestic resilience to sustain front-end repricing. The ECB decision will distinguish imported inflation pressure from credit restraint, while Indonesia's funding exercises will show whether diversification can broaden demand despite currency and external-account strain.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.37% (+2 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.26% (+1 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 37 bp (-2 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 87 bp (-3 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Energy pressure drives a bear flattening as real yields keep the duration hurdle high
overall_summary.session_narrative: The NY close extended the energy-led Treasury selloff, but front-end underperformance changed the curve signal from the prior session's bear steepening to a bear flattening. Oil raised the inflation boundary, while resilient labor conditions and higher real yields strengthened the transmission into policy-sensitive rates. Stronger equities, lower volatility, and stable broad credit limited the move from becoming a generalized tightening shock, even as vulnerable borrowers faced acute refinancing pressure.
overall_summary.summary: Treasuries weakened as persistent oil pressure shifted the marginal adjustment toward the Fed-sensitive front end. Firm real yields reinforced the duration burden, while resilient equities and contained broad credit moves kept the session orderly.

watch_events.market_implication: Resilience sustains front-end vulnerability; weakness would challenge the bear flattening.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad demand resilience with energy still elevated, or weak rate-sensitive data that pull real yields lower.
watch_events.title: US activity data and FOMC decision
watch_events.watch_line: Housing, durable-goods, and forward indicators will test demand resilience before the FOMC.

watch_events.market_implication: Inflation focus adds to the global front-end burden; restraint language supports duration.
watch_events.market_trigger: A prospective increase tied to geopolitical inflation, or greater emphasis on weak loan demand.
watch_events.title: ECB policy and credit-restraint test
watch_events.watch_line: Policy language will show whether energy inflation or existing credit restraint dominates.

watch_events.market_implication: Strong absorption broadens the buyer base; weak execution exposes external funding constraints.
watch_events.market_trigger: Demand near the targeted size without material repricing, versus concession, reduction, or delay.
watch_events.title: Indonesia sovereign funding absorption
watch_events.watch_line: Panda-bond demand and interest in long Danantara notes will test funding diversification.
